schools.summary: Matthew Henry: Agrippa the most honourable person in the assembly (having the title of king), as the mouth of the court giving Paul licence to speak after Festus opened the cause; 'Paul was silent till he had this liberty allowed him, for those are not the most forward to speak that are best prepared to speak and speak best'; Paul stretching forth the hand (the orator's gesture) and beginning with dignity — the order and patience of the apostle (waiting for leave, then speaking with grace), the great audience (preaching to kings, Matthew 10:18), the composure grace gives the prisoner before the king.

themes: thou art permitted to speak for thyself

schools.summary: Gill: a handsome, artful way of introducing his defence and gaining the king's attention, yet not mere flattery — it had been Paul's unhappiness that his case was not understood (neither Lysias nor Felix nor Festus knew the Jewish rites and customs), but Agrippa was master of them, which Paul looked on as in his favour; Barnes: Paul esteemed it a favour to defend before one who could understand the case (turning on Jewish questions, the Messiah, the resurrection); the honest, gracious opening (contrasted with Tertullus's false flattery, Acts 24:2-4), the providence of the knowledgeable judge.

themes: I think myself happy, king Agrippa
glad to plead before one who understood

schools.summary: Barnes: Paul could say this without falsehood or flattery — Agrippa a Jew, having spent much time in the region, acquainted with the customs and questions (the inquiries between Pharisees, Sadducees, scribes); Gill: Agrippa expert in all the rites and ceremonies, whether of the law of Moses or the elders; the honest appeal to the king's knowledge (grounded in fact not flattery), the advantage to Paul's case (the knowledgeable judge able to weigh the Jewish questions fairly), the courteous request to be heard patiently (the apostle confident his case would bear an expert's scrutiny, the truth inviting examination).

themes: expert in all customs and questions among the Jews

schools.summary: Barnes: Paul refers to his opinions, principles, and conduct from his youth (the early part, when habits are formed), born in Tarsus but sent early to Jerusalem for education in the school of Gamaliel (Acts 22:3), his manner of life known to all the Jews; Gill: his conduct and deportment from the time he could perform religious exercises; the appeal to his well-known, open, observed early life among the Jews at Jerusalem (the Jews could attest his strict Pharisee character, no secret heretic) — refuting the charge of being a lifelong enemy of the law (the known life against the slander, the wise grounding in verifiable facts).

themes: my manner of life from my youth
the appeal to his known character

schools.summary: Barnes: the Jews knew Paul from the commencement of his career, perfectly apprised of his whole course; 'the most straitest' (the most rigid, most strict) — not only the written law but the traditions of the elders; Paul elsewhere testifying (Philippians 3:4-6) he had all the advantages of birth and training and distinguished himself by his observance; Paul having lived as a strict Pharisee (no heretic or enemy of the law but the most zealous observer) — the establishing of his former orthodoxy as the foil to his conversion (for one so zealous to become a Christian was no light apostasy but a conviction wrought by God), the unwilling witnesses ('if they would testify').

themes: after the most straitest sect... I lived a Pharisee
which knew me from the beginning

schools.summary: Barnes: Paul arraigned, judged on the point on which all his nation are agreed (the hope of the promise); not quite certain whether he refers to the promise of the Messiah or the hope of the resurrection (before the sanhedrim, Acts 23:6, the hope of the resurrection); Gill: the promise of the Messiah and salvation by him, and the resurrection and eternal glory; Paul on trial for the national hope, not any crime (the charge dissolved into a question of the hope his nation shared) — the irony (the accusers prosecuting him for their own hope), the continuity with the fathers (the gospel the hope of Israel realized, Acts 28:20).

themes: judged for the hope of the promise
made of God unto our fathers
judged for the hope, not a crime

schools.summary: Barnes: 'our twelve tribes' the name by which the Jews were designated, the ancient nation hoping to come to that promise (the hope and expectation of the nation); Gill: the promise of the Messiah, and of the resurrection and eternal glory following; the twelve tribes earnestly serving God day and night in hope of the promise (the devout, persevering national expectation), yet Paul accused for that very hope — the irony (the accusers prosecuting him for the hope they themselves serve God to attain, opposing the fulfilment of their own hope), Paul's faith the realization of the national hope in Christ, the direct appeal to Agrippa.

themes: our twelve tribes... hope to come
instantly serving God day and night
for which hope's sake I am accused

schools.summary: Gill: the resurrection thought incredible by the heathen (laughed at by the Stoics and Epicureans at Athens, Acts 17:32) and by the Sadducees, Paul addressing either Festus and the heathen present or Agrippa and the Sadducees in court, expostulating why it should be looked on as incredible; Barnes: the appeal to the power of God; the resurrection reasonable when God's power is considered (the God who made all things can raise the dead, Genesis 18:14, no harder than creation) — the challenge preparing for the testimony of the risen Christ (v.23), the rebuke of the unbelief that limits God (Matthew 22:29).

themes: why should it be thought incredible
that God should raise the dead

schools.summary: Gill: a correction of himself (why should he wonder at their unbelief when this was once his own case) — the real sentiment of his mind, what in his conscience he believed right; Barnes: Paul begins the account of his conversion, stating he was not originally disposed to be a Christian but violently opposed, thinking he ought to oppose the name of Jesus; the conscientious error (sincere but sincerely wrong, the zeal opposing Christ in good faith, 1 Timothy 1:13) — the foil to the grace that converted him (only grace could turn the sincere opponent), the warning that sincerity alone does not save.

themes: I ought to do many things contrary to the name of Jesus

schools.summary: Gill: in Jerusalem he consented to Stephen's death, held the witnesses' clothes, haled men and women to prison, and made havoc of the church (Acts 8:3); Barnes: 'gave my voice against them' = he voted or concurred in their condemnation; Paul imprisoning the saints by authority of the chief priests and voting for their deaths — the depth of his violent, bloody persecution (Galatians 1:13), owned openly before the king (the honest confession of his great sin, 'the chief of sinners,' 1 Timothy 1:15), the foil to the grace that saved him (the depth of the sin magnifying the mercy), the persecution sanctioned by the religious leaders.

themes: many of the saints did I shut up in prison
I gave my voice against them

schools.summary: Barnes: 'compelled them to blaspheme' = to deny Jesus was the Messiah and reproach him as an impostor (not necessarily implying he succeeded, but that he violently sought it); 'exceedingly mad' = the rage of his persecution; Gill: he punished them by beating and scourging in the synagogues (Acts 22:19); Paul punishing the saints often, seeking to compel blasphemy, and in his fury pursuing them to foreign cities (as Damascus) — the extremity of the persecution (the relentless rage owned candidly, the honest confession of the worst), the foil to the grace that mastered him (the fury overcome by mercy, the grace that tamed the persecutor).

themes: I punished them oft in every synagogue

schools.summary: Gill: being intent on punishing the saints, Paul went to Damascus (the chief city of Syria) where he knew there were many believers, armed with authority from the chief priests; Matthew Henry: those who act by God's direction and warrant are not to be opposed, for that is fighting against God; the persecutor at the height of his enmity (armed with official authority, the sanctioned persecution, the violent mission), about to be met by Christ and converted (the sovereign grace that arrests the enemy on his errand, the dramatic reversal at hand), the persecutor about to learn he fought against the Lord.

themes: authority and commission from the chief priests
the persecutor about to meet Christ

schools.summary: Robertson: 'at midday' (more vivid than the phrase in Acts 22:6), 'above the brightness of the sun' (here alone, not in Acts 9 or 22, indicating the supernatural character of the light); Gill: Paul calls on Agrippa to attend, the matter being wonderful and important; the light at noon brighter than the sun (no natural phenomenon, the glory of the risen Christ) — the supernatural appearance attested by its surpassing the noonday sun (the reality no delusion), seen also by the companions (the event public, corroborated), the glory of the exalted Christ that struck down the persecutor.

themes: a light from heaven, above the brightness of the sun
the glory of Christ that arrested Paul

schools.summary: Ellicott: the genuineness of 'it is hard for thee to kick against the pricks' here beyond doubt; Robertson: the voice spoke 'in the Hebrew language' (Aramaic); the voice of Christ asking 'why persecutest thou me?' (the searching question, the persecution of the church identified with Christ, as Acts 9:4; 22:7) and adding the proverb of the goads — the figure of the futility and self-harm of resisting God (an ox kicking the goad only wounds itself, so Paul resisting the convictions, the dying of Stephen, the truth, only harmed himself), the warning against fighting against God (Acts 5:39), the goad turned to mercy.

themes: Saul, Saul, why persecutest thou me?
it is hard for thee to kick against the pricks

schools.summary: Gill notes the reading 'the Lord said' in some copies; Paul's question 'Who art thou, Lord?' (the awakened conscience), Christ's answer 'I am Jesus whom thou persecutest' — the risen Christ revealing himself as the very Jesus Paul persecuted (the persecutor confronted with the living Lord he opposed, his unbelief shattered, the resurrection proved, 1 Corinthians 15:8), again identifying himself with his persecuted church ('whom thou persecutest,' the Head feeling his members' sufferings, the union of Christ and his church), the grace in the revelation that converts.

themes: I am Jesus whom thou persecutest
the persecutor confronted with the living Lord

schools.summary: Gill: these words (not in the former accounts) bid Saul rise and take heart, for though he had acted so cruelly Christ had designs of grace toward him, the appearance not for his destruction but for his honour and usefulness; Christ raising Paul and appointing him a minister (servant) and witness of what he had seen (the risen Christ) and of what Christ would yet reveal — the commission of the persecutor to the apostleship (grace not only pardoning but employing the enemy, 1 Timothy 1:16, a pattern), the encouragement in the raising (the grace that lifts up the fallen), the honour of the appointment.

themes: rise, and stand upon thy feet
to make thee a minister and a witness

schools.summary: Gill: 'the people' means the people of the Jews (distinguished from the Gentiles), for the Lord knew that as soon as Saul was converted and preached the Jews would become his implacable enemies, so he promises deliverance beforehand; Barnes: this implied Paul would be persecuted by both Jews and Gentiles, and the Lord would rescue him; Christ promising deliverance and sending Paul to the Gentiles (Acts 22:21) — the foreknowledge and provision (forearming his servant with the promise), the protection amid persecution (Acts 18:10), the commission to the nations defining Paul's apostleship, the comfort of the promise.

themes: delivering thee from the people, and from the Gentiles
unto whom now I send thee

schools.summary: Gill expounds 'to open their eyes' — the eyes of the understanding, enlightening them to see their lost state, the sinfulness of sin, and Christ the only Saviour; the great commission summarizing the work of conversion — opening eyes (enlightenment), turning from darkness to light and from the power of Satan to God (conversion, Colossians 1:13), receiving forgiveness of sins (justification) and inheritance among the sanctified (glorification); the fullest statement of the gospel's saving effects, all received 'by faith that is in me' (faith in Christ the means, Romans 5:1), Christ the center (the light, the object of faith, the giver).

themes: to open their eyes... from darkness to light
from the power of Satan unto God
by faith that is in me

schools.summary: Barnes: since the proof of Christ being the Messiah, of his resurrection, and of the calling was so clear, Paul deemed it his duty to engage without delay; 'I was not disobedient' = he was not unbelieving but yielded himself and obeyed at once; Gill: this followed the illustrious appearance and commission; Paul's prompt, full obedience to the heavenly vision (the will surrendered, the clear proof requiring obedience, Acts 9:6) — the rebel turned servant (the obedience replacing the opposition), the obedience his defence before Agrippa (he could do no other, having to obey the divine vision).

themes: I was not disobedient unto the heavenly vision

schools.summary: Barnes: this verse a summary of his labours in obedience to the command, his argument that the Lord had commanded him and he had done no more than obey; Clarke: he preached at Damascus and Arabia about three years before going to Jerusalem (Galatians 1:17-18); Paul preaching repentance and turning to God, with works meet for (worthy of) repentance, to Damascus, Jerusalem, Judea, and the Gentiles — the message of conversion and its fruit (the changed life proving the repentance real, Matthew 3:8, works the fruit and evidence not the ground), the order of the mission (the Jew first, then the Gentile, Romans 1:16), the ministry the fulfilment of the vision.

themes: the fruit that proves the root

schools.summary: Gill: Paul means the Asiatic Jews, who finding him in the temple laid hold on him and went about to kill him, for no other reason but his preaching to the Gentiles and the doctrines mentioned; Clarke: the causes reduce to his maintaining the resurrection and preaching to the Gentiles; the true ground of the assault the gospel itself (Paul seized not for any crime but for preaching repentance, the resurrection, the Gentile mission) — the persecution for the truth (2 Timothy 3:12), the consequence of his obedience to the commission (vv.19-20), the offence of the Gentile mission to the Jewish exclusivism (Acts 22:21-22).

themes: the Jews caught me in the temple

schools.summary: Gill: God had delivered Paul many times from Jews and Gentiles according to his promise (v.17), saving him from all their designs, so he could say he continued to this day; Barnes: Paul witnessed to small and great; Paul attributing his preservation to God's help (the divine preservation, kept for the work, 2 Timothy 4:17) and declaring he taught nothing but what Moses and the prophets foretold (the suffering and resurrection of the Messiah, v.23, the light to the Gentiles) — the gospel the fulfilment of the Scriptures (Acts 10:43, no novelty but the fulfilment, the continuity vindicating it against the charge of heresy).

themes: having obtained help of God, I continue
witnessing both to small and great
none other things than Moses and the prophets
the gospel the fulfilment of Scripture

schools.summary: Gill: 'that Christ should suffer' recorded by Moses and the prophets (the meaning of the passover, the brazen serpent, the sacrifices, the constant account of the prophets), the sufferer Christ in his human nature though sufferings ascribed to his whole person; the threefold substance of the prophetic gospel — Christ should suffer (Isaiah 53:6), be the first to rise from the dead (the firstfruits, 1 Corinthians 15:20), and show light to the people and the Gentiles (Luke 2:32; Isaiah 49:6) — foretold by Moses and the prophets (the offence answered by their own Scriptures, the Gentile mission foretold), Christ the center of the gospel.

themes: the first that should rise from the dead
light unto the people, and to the Gentiles

schools.summary: Matthew Henry: Paul likely had much more to say in defence of the gospel, but Festus interrupted him; Barnes: the happy turn Paul gives this accusation — Festus, a heathen, hearing the doctrines of the resurrection and the light to the Gentiles, thought Paul's much learning had made him mad; the natural man counting the gospel folly (Festus unable to follow the spiritual truths — the resurrection, the suffering and risen Messiah — beyond the carnal mind, 1 Corinthians 2:14; 1:18), the irony (the most reasonable of men charged with madness), the contrast with Agrippa (the heathen's verdict against the Jew's conviction, v.28).

themes: much learning doth make thee mad
the natural man counting the gospel folly
the spiritual truths beyond the carnal mind

schools.summary: Gill: Paul gave Festus his title of honour, not out of fear or flattery but according to custom, though Festus used him reproachfully; Barnes: the happy turn — Paul might have dwelt on his argument but instead calmly affirms his words are truth and soberness; Paul calmly, courteously denying madness ('most noble Festus') and asserting he speaks 'words of truth and soberness' (the gospel not madness but rational, sound truth, against the charge) — the composure of the apostle (grace under accusation, the meek answer, 2 Timothy 2:25), the rationality of the gospel (reasonable, well-grounded), the contrast of the verdicts.

themes: I am not mad, most noble Festus
the rational sober truth of the gospel

schools.summary: Gill: the king knew something of the sufferings and resurrection of the Messiah, and of his showing light to Jews and Gentiles as spoken by Moses and the prophets and reported fulfilled in Jesus (at least he had heard the report); Barnes: Agrippa, many years in the region, knew of Jesus and of Paul's conversion; Paul appealing to Agrippa's knowledge, speaking freely (the bold speech of the confident), since the gospel facts were public, notorious, 'not done in a corner' (the open, attested, verifiable events, the gospel grounded in public history) — the appeal from the ignorant Festus to the knowing Agrippa.

themes: the king knoweth of these things
this thing was not done in a corner
the appeal to the king's knowledge
the public facts of the gospel

schools.summary: Barnes: this bland personal address an instance of Paul's happy manner of appeal, done to bring in Agrippa's testimony to meet Festus's charge of derangement; 'believest thou the prophets?' (the prophecies of the Messiah's character, sufferings, death); 'I know that thou believest' (Agrippa, a Jew, believed the prophets); the bold, personal appeal driving the argument home to the king's conscience (if Agrippa believed the prophets, who foretold the suffering, risen Messiah, vv.22-23, he must consider whether Jesus fulfilled them) — the wisdom building on common ground (from the accepted to the contested).

themes: King Agrippa, believest thou the prophets?

schools.summary: Gill takes it spoken seriously rather than ironically, arising from convictions of mind Agrippa could not stifle nor conceal; Barnes: Agrippa could not deny he believed the prophets, nor their fulfilment in Jesus, nor the evidence of Paul's conversion, and candidly avows the impression, 'almost' (en oligō, within a little); the famous near-persuasion (the conviction real, the king moved, near the kingdom, Mark 12:34, yet stopping short of conversion) — the tragedy of the 'almost' (convicted yet not converted), the hindrances holding Agrippa back (his sins, pride, position), the solemn moment of decision passed by, the warning to all almost persuaded.

debates.question: Was Agrippa's 'almost thou persuadest me' serious or ironic?

sides.position: Serious — arising from convictions of mind he could not stifle (the candid avowal of the impression the proof made)

themes: almost thou persuadest me to be a Christian

schools.summary: Barnes: Paul's intense desire that Agrippa and all who heard might be saved, and his steady belief that none but God could incline them to become Christians (he sought it of God earnestly); Gill: the prayer shows his affection for souls and desire for their conversion; Paul answering Agrippa's 'almost' (v.28) with the wish that all be 'altogether' Christians (pressing beyond the near-persuasion to the full conversion), the love embracing every hearer (Romans 10:1), the gracious exception ('except these bonds,' wishing them his faith and joy but not his sufferings), the conversion God's work ('I would to God').

themes: both almost, and altogether such as I am
the wish for all to be Christians

schools.summary: Chrysostom: again they pass sentence in his favour — having said 'thou art beside thyself,' they now acquit him as undeserving not only of death but of bonds, and would have released him entirely had he not appealed to Caesar; but this was done providentially, that he should also depart with bonds (for if his Lord was reckoned among the transgressors, Mark 15:28; Isaiah 53:12, much more he, the fellowship of his sufferings, Philippians 3:10); the king, governor, Bernice, and assembly rising (the hearing ended, the favourable verdict); the providence in the appeal to Caesar carrying the gospel to Rome (Acts 23:11), the marvel of Paul mixed with the great yet untainted.

themes: the king rose up, and the governor, and Bernice

schools.summary: Barnes: the conclusion after hearing all the Jews alleged — the concurring testimony of Claudius Lysias (Acts 23:29), Felix (Acts 24), Festus (Acts 25:26-27), and now Agrippa to Paul's innocence (more honourable and satisfactory testimony Paul could not have desired, a full acquittal from all the charges); the king, governor, and assembly agreeing in private that Paul had done nothing worthy of death or bonds — the full vindication of the apostle (the repeated concurring verdicts, the innocence established, Psalm 37:6), the gospel vindicated in its preacher, the innocent yet still bound (the appeal to Caesar carrying him to Rome).

themes: this man doeth nothing worthy of death or of bonds

schools.summary: Barnes: a full declaration of Agrippa's conviction that Paul was innocent, an instance where boldness and fidelity are attended with happy results (Paul had concealed nothing, made a bold appeal to Agrippa, v.27, who was not offended but the more impressed with his innocence); Gill: Agrippa spoke his sense by way of advice, the matter resting with Festus; Paul might have been freed but for his appeal to Caesar (Acts 25:11) — the final testimony to his innocence, yet the providence of the appeal carrying him to Rome (Acts 23:11, the chains the path to the wider witness, the seeming loss the divine gain, the journey ordained, Acts 27).

themes: might have been set at liberty, if he had not appealed unto Caesar
